No matter where you are today in your business and in your life, you're just one funnel away. Hey everyone, my name is Russell Brunson. I want to welcome you to the Funnel Hacker Cookbook today. We are actually filming this in front of a live studio audience, which I'm really excited about because we want to share with you guys really everything you need to understand funnels. And it's kind of interesting if you're a member of ClickFunnels, you're probably watching this, maybe you've read one or two of my books. I had a lot of friends who have written weight loss books and diet books and things like that. And they always write a diet book and then the second book that they write is always a cookbook. I was like, that's so cool. I wish that I could do a cookbook, but I'm a marketing dude. I don't have anything to do. And then one day after I finished the dot com secrets book and the expert secrets book, which are all strategy, and then I was like, what if we had a cookbook? And all of a sudden I was like, what if we did have a cookbook? We could call it, we had all these names. And all of a sudden it was like, we call it the Funnel Hacker Cookbook. And that's where its name came from. And literally it's been about three or four months since we had the idea and we've been killing ourselves to put this whole thing together. And what started as an idea has now turned into a 350 plus page actual cookbook, which we're really excited for. So this is what the cookbook is. And what it really is, it's kind of like, again, in the weight loss market, if you're ready to diet book, you're like, oh, I understand the strategy behind the diet, why I should do it. And then you get the cookbook to understand the recipe, so how you actually cook. And so this cookbook is kind of the same thing. It's a cookbook, so you've got the recipes and actually how to build these funnels. Everyone's popping up all over the place as funnel consultants. It's hard because they don't know what they're talking about. Do they not? I feel like every entrepreneur, every business owner needs to understand the basics because if you don't, even if you hire someone else to do it, you can get in a lot of trouble. So it's important to understand these things. Now the other thing is that, whereas I'm not selling this book or this training series you're going through right now, I'm giving this away because I want everyone to use it. In fact, on the page, if you're at funnelhackercookbook.com, you're watching this video down below, there will be a link where you can literally just download the PDF and print it out. Like I'm not charging for this. I want you guys all to have it. Or if you want a physical copy like this that you can open up and actually have on your desks while you're working, there will be a link where you can buy that. I think our hard costs are like $10 per book plus shipping. So you're just covering the hard costs and we'll ship you out a book as well because I just want everyone to have this. And I said this video training, I'm going to be going over the next three or four hours. I promise you that what you'll learn from this process is more than you would learn from probably any marketing funnel training, whatever course that a lot of other people are selling for $1,000, $2,000, $5,000 and above. And we're doing this because all the other people that are teaching this stuff, like they make their money by teaching it, we make our money when you use click funnels. And so I'm giving this away because I want you to be better at building funnels and better at using click funnels. And that's kind of my evil, my evil ulterior motive is to give you the best training on planet earth. So use our software so you make a lot of money so you change the world. So I hope that's okay with everyone who's listening. So that's kind of the game plan. Now I'm going to be going through a couple of different things as I go through this. First I want to talk about some funnel strategy because I want to make sure that everyone understands the core basics. A lot of people who come to click funnels or start businesses, they know traditional stuff but then they hear about funnels, they start understanding it and they're not really sure like, why do I need a funnel? How does this work for me? And what's the difference between a funnel and a website? And I want to go through the strategy here first because if you understand the strategy, then all the pieces, all of the stuff that comes with the cookbook and which funnels and when we use each funnels, all that stuff will make perfect sense as we get to it. So that is kind of the game plan. So the first thing I want to talk about here is what is an actual funnel, right? That's probably the question that I've gotten the most in the last three or so years since we launched click funnels. What is a funnel? Do I need one? And sometimes the easiest way to explain what a funnel is is kind of talking about what it's not because what people think it is a lot of times is, oh, it's a website. And so I took a picture here of what a traditional website probably looks like. And most business owners, this is what we know, right? This is what people have been doing forever. It makes me laugh if you look back 15 years ago when I started this business, when I was three, that back then everyone I remember was getting on, like the internet was coming around, everyone's like, do I need a website? And it's funny looking back now because everyone's like, well, of course you need a website. That's how people buy stuff nowadays. Back then it was a new thing and that's kind of where the future is going. Everyone's asking, do I need a funnel? I don't understand. Like why do I need this? But in the future it'll only be funnels. Like that's what I want people to understand. This is a dead model. And if it's not dead, it's dying in almost every market. So this is what a traditional website looks like, right? Designer designs it. It's beautiful. It looks awesome. You put it out there and it looks awesome and then you send people to it and what happens? You buy ads on Facebook, on YouTube, on Google. They come to this page. They see your logo. They see your little slider bar. They see all your flashing images and all the cool stuff the designer put in there. But it literally is like slamming a whole bunch of people into a brick wall. Like boom, they come here and nothing happens. If you look at a traditional website, most websites that people have me review have anywhere from like 30 to 100 different call to actions on the page. You come and there's a menu and the menu's got a sub menu and that means you've got another menu and then you've got 40 different things on the page. And it's just like you're literally taking someone and shoving into the thing and like, hey, make as many choices as you want and hopefully they give me money at the end. Okay. So there's a phrase in marketing that says, the confused mind will always say no. And so this is confusing. It's literally taking all your customers and slamming them into a brick wall. And so that's what people have been doing and they don't know any better. So that's what they do. And they're like, well, my website, I asked people all the time, like, was your website like, is it making you money? And they're like, well, I don't know. It's there. Like people see it and then they call us some day. I'm like, I don't think that's what's happening. People call you. They don't even see this. If they do see this, they're leaving. And so it actually, a lot of times, hurts businesses more than it helps it. So that's kind of what a traditional website is. The sales funnel now is the evolution of that. Like I was, I almost look at this, if you were to try to build a sales team, right? And you hire a sales person and you'd build this beautiful thing that looks awesome and you'd give them a brochure and the sales person had people walk by and they'd hand the brochure to him. And then just like, watch person walk past and like, hopefully they'll come back. I hope they come back. That'd be awesome. And the next person comes in and hand them a brochure. That's how a lot of people are doing their online sales, right? Sales person's handing out brochures, praying they come back. Now, if I had a sales person who handed out brochures and prayed they'd come back, I would fire him, right? It's like the worst sales model ever. So sales funnels work more traditionally like an actual human. Like it's the way that people want to interact and experience with you and your company. So a sales funnel takes people through a process, right? So it drives ads and it says slamming them into a brick wall. I take them to a page that has something, some kind of lead capture. We'll talk about these, these are the different types of pages we'll go into deeper here in a minute. We take them through and we capture the lead, right? Just like if someone was to walk in my store and I was a sales person and I greeted them, I would take them and like, hey, welcome to our store and I would capture them as the lead. And then I would find out like, what do they actually want? And I've talked to them and I figure out what they want. And I go, oh cool, in my store, this is what you need to buy. I take them to that thing. And then I'm like, well, what else would it be you're trying to do? Like, oh, you're trying to make a cake? Well, you also need this and this and what I would make the most perfect buying experience for them possible as I took them through the store and eventually up to the checkout stand where they bought the product or the service. If you do it right, a good sales person makes the experience a million times better than slamming someone into a brick wall. Okay? Like that's just, it's hard to beat a brick wall experience, but that's kind of what you do. And so that's kind of what a sales funnel is from a hierarchy standpoint. Now there's a lot that goes into that and I'm going to kind of go through it, but that's kind of the difference between a website and an actual funnel. Okay? All right, now the next thing I want you to understand, this comes back to the strategy of like some of the core things like, like most people that in business don't understand these things and if they understood them, then all these funnel things start making a ton of sense. So when I got started in my business, one of my first mentors was a guy named Dan Kennedy and he said something that was huge to me. He said, whoever can spend the most money to acquire a customer wins. And honestly at first I didn't really know what he meant, but he kept saying like, whoever can spend the most money to acquire customer wins. And I remember thinking, I'm like, well, I'm in trouble because I don't have that much money. And I'm looking at people I'm competing against and there's huge companies like Walmart and Amazon and like, you know, all these people have tons of money. I'm like, if that's true, whoever can spend the most money to acquire customer wins, like I'm kind of in trouble because I don't have that much money. Like I'm going to lose and this whole business is going to go under. And so it didn't make sense to me at first when he first started to tell me this, but he kept saying, I'm like, there's something there, but I don't, I don't really get it yet. So as I started good into business, started having some successes and some huge failures, it started making sense to me. So this is, those of you who've ever heard me talk before, I talk about this a lot, but one of my very first products I ever created was an amazing DVD teaching people how to make potato guns. It was funny. I actually found the original footage from the very first time we shot the potato gun we made just the other day, like on an old like tiny camera thing. So I was watching with my wife the other day, which was really fun. But this is my first thing. I'm from Boise, Idaho. And as everyone here noticed, we have potatoes everywhere on the ground. And so for fun, we don't really. So for fun, we shoot potatoes here in Idaho, right? So anyway, as I was learning and marketing, I remember I made a potato gun one weekend and I did all the research online. I found the plans and all sorts of stuff like that. And I was like, I wonder if anyone else is searching for information on potato guns. Found out they were. So my buddy and I, this is Nate Plone right here. So Nate and I went to Home Depot with a handy cam. We filmed the whole DVD teaching people how to make potato guns, put it up online and I started to sell my potato gun, my first like little information product. And the way it worked back then, it was really fun. Everything we did were Google ads. So I go to Google and I buy an ad and I'd send it to buy my potato gun DVD, which was 37 bucks. Okay. Now on average, I was spending about $10 a day in ads. And for the $10 a day, I'd average about one sell a day. Now as a college kid, this is my graduation picture. Yay. That was a lot of money. 27 bucks a day profit for a kid like me was like, I was the richest kid in school, right? And it was like an ATM machine. I put $10 in ads to Google and then someone would buy a thing. And for 37 bucks, I get 27. I'm like, huh. And I kept having this little thing happening. And it was like the greatest, the coolest thing in the world. I thought I was like on top of the world. I remember this about time that all like the dot com things were happening. I was like, oh my gosh, like I figured out I'm a genius. And then something really sad happened. Google shifted their algorithm, the dot com bubble pop for me. Now I know that for me, it was a big tragedy, but I'm sure it was worse for other people, but I didn't really know what happened. All I knew was that one day, I was sending the exact same amount of clicks to my little website here, but the cost went from $10 a day to $50 a day overnight. Boom. And I was like, what just happened? And I'd still make the same one sell a day, but I spent $50. I'd make 37. I would lose $13 a day. After about four or five days of this, my beautiful wife was like, you can't do this very much longer. Your business is bad. And I was like, oh yeah, it is really bad. All these, yeah, it doesn't last very long. And so this time was really sad time. We actually had credit cards. This is a real picture of us credit cards. This is my beautiful wife crying. This is her failed husband being like, my business failed. I got to go back to school and figure out what I'm going to do with my life, which was really depressing because I thought I'd figured it out. And so that was what was happening. And I had a couple websites like that that were making me money, and then they all just overnight were gone. And I was like, great, I missed it. I got to figure out how to start making money again. And then a little while later, one of my friends came up to me, and he had a similar business to mine. He had a bunch of little DVDs and things he was selling. He's like, Russell, I figured it out. I'm like, what do you mean? He's like, check this out. I started adding, I remember he called him OTO. I was like, what's an OTO? And he said, it's like an upsell. He says OTO stands for one time offer. Somebody bought my thing, and I was like, hey, I have a special one time offer for you right now where you can get something else. I was like, OK, how does that make you money? And he's like, well, it's like McDonald's. And he explained it. I didn't know this at the time. But McDonald's actually, I don't know the exact numbers off the top of my head, but they spend $2 or $3 to get somebody to come into the drive-thru. Now if you buy a hamburger, it's like $1.50. So they actually lose money if you just come to the drive-thru and buy a hamburger. But then after they buy a hamburger, they're like, hey, do you want fries and a Coke with that? And they're like, oh, sure. They lost him nothing. We got potatoes laying everywhere out here like I told you. They just grabbed it, chopped it up, they got fries. And Coke's like, it's so cheap, but they charge like $2 or $3 for that. And the majority of people take the fries and the Coke, and all of a sudden, they're profitable. The upsells is what makes McDonald's profitable. That's how they became McDonald's. They understand that whoever can spend the most money to acquire a customer wins. They understood that, and then I didn't. And my friend understood. He added these upsells, and he said, right now, he said for every one of his DVDs he had, he had a bigger product. He understood that about 20 to 30% of the people who bought the first thing started buying the next thing. I was like, that's cool. But how would that work for my potato gun thing? All I got is a potato gun DVD. I don't have any upsells. He's like, we need to find out, create something. I was like, okay, what should I do? And he was like, well, do you know how to, what do people want? What's the biggest concern? And as you'll find out, this whole thing with business is all about figuring out what people want. And I'm like, well, the biggest pain is they buy the DVD, but then they got to go home deep on them, buy the pipes, and cut the pipes. And there's a lot of annoying stuff that goes with it. In fact, most of the refunds we get, people are like, I don't have the time to go buy all the pipes. Give me my 37 bucks back. And so I was like, if I could find someone who could make a potato gun kit and send it to him, that would take all my problems away. And so I did some research online. I found a guy in Northern Idaho who actually makes potato gun kits. I kid you not, it's a real thing. And I contacted him, like, do you wholesale like your potato gun kits? He's like, I've never had someone ask me that before. I'm like, I'm sure you have it. I want to sell these on my website. And he's like, sure, whatever. So we figured out a deal. We started selling these potato gun kits as my upsell. So I set the whole thing up, got it ready, got it live. And I turned the ads back on in Google to see what was going to happen. And this is kind of what the page looked like. So it went from just a static web page that someone bought and then we upsold the potato gun kit. And that's how we transitioned from a funnel, excuse me, from a website into a funnel. Now here's the math behind it. What happened, they went back to Google, flipped the switch back on, turned ads on, and they still kept charging me 50 bucks a day. Those rich punks, they are not nice to little broke people like me. They don't care. They just want our money, right? So taking all my money, 50 bucks a day. I still on average made one sell a day. But what was cool is that one out of three people bought the $197 upsell. So not everybody, but one out of three. So you do the math on that. This is my mathematical abilities. Three divided by 197 is 65. So for every DVD I sold, I averaged an extra $65 in profit, which is pretty cool. So total each day I was averaging about $102 in sell. So I was spending $50 and I was making $102. Now that starts turning back into a business. Okay? So you look at the math, they'd buy this, one-third of it by this, and I was averaging net profit in my pocket as a college kid, $52 per day. And I was like, oh my gosh, this is the key. This is everything. And what I found from this experience is that funnels actually made me money and websites made me broke. And I was like, this is the future. This is like 15 years ago. I was like, oh my gosh, I figured out something nobody knows about. And there were a group of people like me who were obsessed with it. And literally I went and I started launching funnels in all sorts of businesses. They were in the dating market, diabetes market, weight loss, couponing, like on, on, on, on, on, on, all these different businesses. Because I figured like that was the trick. Because I could spend more, just like we talked about before, I could spend more than anybody else. I could go in these markets and compete. In fact, here it is right here. Whoever can spend again, whoever can spend the most money to acquire customer wins. I was able to spend more because I had an upsell. As soon as that happened, I could turn my ads back on. I could outspend every single person out there. Okay? If you look at our company ClickFunnels, the reason why we have grown as fast as we can, as we have is because I can literally outspend everyone right now. Okay? I can spend more than anybody else. Right now, this book that we sell, okay? The expert secrets book. I think on average right now, we average $36 in, in the upsell, downsells from every single person who buys that book. Okay? This is why this is important. I can spend up to $36 to sell my book and still break even. Now, every other business author I'm competing against, okay? They're selling their book for $9.95 on Amazon Kindle or maybe $19.95 to get the book shipped out to them. Most they can spend is $20 to break even and they still got to pay Kindle and Amazon, all these other things, right? So I can spend twice as much to sell every one of my books as all these guys so I can sell way more books than everybody else. Right? We had a really cool experience the other day. Since ClickFunnels has been growing, we've had a whole bunch of people, venture capitalists and equity people, people who have money who want to come and they want to acquire ClickFunnels. We always kind of laugh at them because that's not part of our business plan. We like controlling the company, but we always humor them because it's fun to hear their story and what they want and things like that. Dave and I actually had a chance to go to dinner with one of them and we're sitting down and he's going through everything and he's looking at it from his investor lenses and he said, okay, well, how much does it cost you guys to acquire a customer? How many of you guys watch Shark Tank? It's a question that I was asking. How much does it cost to acquire a customer? Like 90% of the sharks are like, the attendees are like, I don't know. They never know, right? But I know that it's a number that we look at. How much does it cost to acquire a customer, right? So we're at this VC meeting and the guy asks us, like, well, how much does it cost to acquire a customer? We said, well, right now it's costing us like $120 in ads to get somebody to sign up for the free trial and click funnels. And he's like, are you serious? I'm like, yeah. And I thought, I'm like, that was really bad. He's like, that's amazing. So $120 per customer is like $50 million to give you $30 million. That means you could grow your customers from here. He was all excited like getting like all the numbers, right? I was like, wait, wait, wait, wait, wait, wait, wait, wait, wait, wait. Just real quick, we turned those ads off. And he was looking at me like, why would you turn those ads off? You're a moron. I'm like, because this is, like, this is me. Like, I had to pull this money out of my wallet and spend it every single day. I'm not getting you to like finance this whole thing for me for percentage of my company. I'm paying this money myself. He's like, well, how are you guys growing so much? He's like, I don't understand. You guys are such a unicorn. They kept calling us a unicorn. I'm like, well, let me explain how this works. And I walked him through and said, what we do is we have these front end funnels where somebody will buy a book teaching what our software does and they pay for the book and then we have these upsells and these down. So I showed him the whole process and I said, for everyone of these books, we make whatever it is. Each book's different. $32, $36 we sell the book for and it costs us anywhere from $5 to $20 on Facebook to sell the book. So we spend, let's say, we spend $20 to sell a book. We average $32. There's $12 in the middle we get as cash we put in our pocket. And then we introduce that person to click funnels for free. So I said, every single person who introduced to click funnels has actually paid us net $12 in our pocket before we introduced it to him. He's like, wait, wait, wait, that makes no sense. Start over. So I re-explained the whole thing to him again. Maped it all out, drew little boxes and little arrows. He's like, that doesn't make sense. Do it again. So I did it again. That's the third time he looked at me and he said, man, he said, if what you said is true, if you're really able to do that, he's like, you will change businesses as we know it. He's like, yeah, that's what we're doing. Like that's what we're trying to teach all these entrepreneurs. I can show you tens of thousands of entrepreneurs right now, just like you were watching this video, who came into a market, you have huge competitors you're going against and you come in and you're thrashing them because of these concepts. When we started ClickFunnels, we had two big competitors, one of them had taken on over $40 million in funding, one taken on $100 million in funding. That's who I was competing against. We're not going to beat that from a cash standpoint. We are going to be from a funnel standpoint. And it blows my mind that no one on their teams have seen this and modeled it. It blows my minds. But that's how we're doing it. So that's what I want you guys to understand, that whoever can spend the most money to acquire customer wins, and when you understand these funnels and how they work, that's the key. That's how you do it. All right. So I want to share a couple more things with you guys, kind of set this foundation before we dive into the actual Lego blocks here and start building them out. So I want to share with you guys kind of the history of growth hacking. And this ties into funnels. And it makes me laugh. Growth hacking is kind of the big term nowadays. Everyone's like, oh, it's growth hacking. And so I bought a bunch of books on growth hacking. And I started reading these books. And what made me laugh was they're basically just teaching the same internet marketing things we've been doing for the last decade. And that was like this cool new thing in San Francisco. It's like, we call this growth hacking. Like, oh, we call internet marketing. We've been doing it for a decade. And it works really, really good. So they're all catching up to us now. You guys, because you're here, are like a decade ahead of everyone over in Silicon Valley. So congratulations. So I want to give you a history lesson on what's been happening over the last decade so you guys all see how it works. OK? So this is how businesses were created. This is about the same time my potato gun stuff was happening. This is one of my friends, Evan Pagan. He had a company called Double Your Dating. Some of you guys may have heard of Double Your Dating. Became one of the, I think it was the biggest dating company, dating information products that he eventually sold the company. But if you look at the way the business worked is he had a big, huge, long sales letter and he drove traffic to that sales letter. That's how it kind of worked. OK? And his goal was like, he'd buy ads from Google and all different places you could buy ads back then. They'd drive it here. And his goal was trying to get 3 to 5% of the people to buy this product. If he did that, as long as you're spending less to acquire customers, you'd make money. Like, that's how you launch a business. Right? So that was business 14, 15 years ago when I was getting in this. So that's everyone did. You had a sales letter and he drove traffic to you. And that was it. And then things started happening and people started getting smarter. And I don't know if Edmund invented this. I've heard a lot of people claim they invented this. I don't really care. I'm just grateful that whoever did did. But they took this thing and said, OK, this is good, but man, there's 93, 95, 97% of the people who come to this page that just leave. And we spent money to get them there, but we don't get anything from it. And they're like, man, there's got to be a way to figure this out and change things. And so what they, once somebody invented it was a thing called a squeeze page. And this is one of the very first squeeze pages ever. Squeeze page and call them opt-in page. People have a lot of different names for them. But it's basically, it's a page where somebody comes here and they give the name and the email address first. After you email address, then you send them to the page to sell them stuff. And the strategy, the thought behind this is that they found from the test. You can come back to 2% or 3% of people were buying from this page originally. So they found that for somebody to buy your product on average. I don't know where this study is or how accurate, but I do believe it's true based on everything we've done over the last decade. Is it take someone more than once to see your thing on average before they're willing to pull their credit card out and give you money? Do you guys agree with that? Like, do I trust this person? I'm not sure. Let me check it out for a couple of days and we'll see. So what this did is someone come here, they wanted to learn the secrets that most men will never know about women. They're like, what is that? And they get an email address and they get the thing in the email that tells them about it and they introduce them here and they see the page the first time. But now they have their email address. So now they can start following up with them. So Evan can send an email on day one saying, here's the secret I told you about. Now come over here and check out the product. And they do like, hey, you didn't buy the product? Okay, this is another reason why you should buy the product. And gain the ability to follow them seven, 10, 20, 50, 100 times, following up then to close the sale. Okay, in traditional sales, like good sales people don't just come in, hand a brochure and let them walk away, right? They get the contact information, they leave, they're calling back, they're following up, trying to figure out like, why didn't you buy? Let me see if I can resolve your concerns. And suddenly by adding this one step, creating this funnel, they were able to do that. Okay, because there's no physical sales person here on the page answering every single question. So say, so people can figure out like, what are our top 10 concerns? Why people aren't buying? And then one email can be like, here's concern number one. Is this your concern? Let me explain it. And then go back here and buy this thing. And suddenly you're able to take your conversions from two or three or four percent conversion rate and over a period of seven, 10, 20, 30 days, get that to 5%, 10%, 15%. And suddenly every single penny you were spending on ads just became more three, five, 10 times as much. And that's how funnels started evolving, right? And then after this happened, then the next phase was coming back in, adding up sales, like we talked about earlier with potato guns and stuff. And the goal of this was to increase the average cart value. Now there's a couple of mathematical terms we're going to focus on. And I'm not a math guy, so there's only like two and we'll stop with that. But this is an important one. Average cart value, ACV. I'll explain what this is in a minute, but it's how much money and average you make for every single person who goes through your shopping cart. So someone came here, they gave Evan his email address. They came here, some of them bought the book and those who bought the book, some of them bought this upsell here. Now it's his funnel. Built a $20 million company off the back of that. Okay, huge. The key here is that the upsells started increasing average cart value. So this is where over the last decade, this growth hacking in our marketing kind of came from. Is it people taking these things like, well, what happens if I had this? What if I had this? What if I change this? And based on those all sorts of types of funnels have kind of come out of the back of it. And so a lot of times nowadays we get here and it's so complex. You're like, I understand this, but I'm a dentist. Should I do that? Like, I'm a network marketer. Should I do this? And so there's all this confusion because there's so many ways to do funnels. And so my goal with this is to really break it down to you and see like, oh, this is how the pieces all fit together. And give you guys the tools you need to be able to create funnels really, really simply and really easy. Okay. So average cart value, and come back to this again. So average cart value is how much money you make on average when somebody goes through this process immediately, like point of sale. Okay. So I'm at the grocery store. I bought some, some whatever, some cookies and I go out and then I go to the checkout sign and I see there's like, there's got my buy. Maybe there's a Twix and I grab the magazine. Like all that stuff's like happening in the cart. Right. And so the same thing here is like, they buy the book, they buy the upsell, they buy the down. So this all happens within like a three to five minute period of time. And I'm trying to say on average for every single person who buys this, how much am I making on average? So obviously maybe 20% by this, maybe 10% by this. If I do the math, what's the average? Okay. So that's what I'm trying to figure out. So for example, this one right here, I think the last, it's been a while since I've looked, I think the dot com secrets book was around $32, maybe 30 bucks, whatever it was, it was our average cart value. So every book we sold the average about $30 at the same. Okay. So the next thing you look at is where are you buying ads from? So let's just say Facebook, for example, you're buying ads from there. The second math terms again, there's only two. The second one is called CPA cost per acquisition. All it means is how much money do I get? Got to give Mark Zuckerberg to sell one of my things right here. Okay. That's it. So that's as mathematical as you guys gotta be, be funnel builders. Okay. So what's my average? How much do I make when everyone buys this? How much do I give Zuckerberg to get one customer? And then my goal is as long as I'm paying Zuckerberg less than I'm making or making more over here than the metrics work, right? So for example, in ours, in this case, we make $30 per book over here. Maybe we spend $12 to sell books. We sell 12, we make $32, the rest of it goes in my pocket. Boom, and I got a customer. Okay. So the goal with all these funnels is create something where you put a dollar in advertising in and you get $2 back out. That's the goal. Okay. If you've done that, then this is how you can outspend everybody else. People always ask me, well, my funnel is done, what should my budget be? I'm like, if you do it right, you don't have a budget. You have an ATM machine. You're just dumping cash in and you put a dollar and you get $2 back out. I had some of my officers today who worked with someone on a project. They were in a $1.5 million check to this person ahead of time for the marketing budget. The person took that money and lit it on fire, not literally, but pretty close. It was gone. He came to us and he's like, what should we do? And I was like, well, first thing is don't spend $1.5 million. Set up your funnel, spend $100 with Zuckerberg and see if it works. If you get at least $100 back, it's a good sign. You should keep working on this funnel and make it better. If you get zero back, stop the ads. Don't keep burning money. But if you put a dollar and you get $2 back out, you put a dollar and you get $6 back out to dump some more money in. We never spend money and this is profitable. That's the rule. That's the goal with all these funnel things. So that's a big piece for everyone to understand. So that's the mathematic terms. Cost per acquisition, average card value. Now you got that, your mathematical funnel, ninja, wizards. That's all you need to know. All right, next I want to talk about. This came from actually my dot com secrets book for those who have had a chance to read it. I talked a lot about funnel strategy and so I wanted to go a little more in here as well because this will help you to understand how all these pieces here as we start going into how they kind of fit in together. A lot of times we think of funnel like, oh, I need a book funnel. I need a webinar funnel. It's like kind of like it's bigger than that when you understand the strategy. So if you look at this, there's seven phases of a funnel. The first phase starts over here and this is called the traffic temperature. Where are you getting traffic from? This is what a lot of people make mistakes is that they'll take their existing customers who love them and they'll sell something like sweet, my customers love it. I made a ton of money and then they'll go on Facebook or they go to Google and they'll dump in like $1,000 in advertising and it burns up instantly. They don't make a single sale. They're like, why didn't this work? It doesn't make any sense. It's like, well, your warm audience, they love you. You don't have to do that much. If you just tell them like, hey, I got a cool product, they're probably going to give you money. That's for your hot audience. Your warm audience is going to be harder and colder. So you have to speak to people differently depending on who they are. This is a graph to kind of illustrate that. If you look at this, the hot traffic, these are people who know who you are. They're very aware of who you are, what your products are, and they love you. Because of that, they're probably going to buy your stuff. These are the easiest people to sell. The warm market, these are people who are in your industry, but they don't know you yet. Right? Like this, I always look at Facebook ads kind of here in the middle. They're warm traffic. For example, somebody made No Tony Robbins, but they don't know me, but I can buy ads to Tony Robbins people. So those are warm people and I'm going to bring them over to me. Right? That's warm traffic. Back here is freezing cold traffic. People have no idea who you are. They're not even in the industry. They don't even know what in the world the funnel is and if I'm trying to sell to them, it's going to be really, really hard. So I have to sell these people differently. So depending on where I'm getting my traffic or my people into my funnels, the entrance points to my funnels are going to be different. Okay? This is one of the big secret ah-has. People think I build a funnel and I drive traffic. No, you got to understand as you're building it, my building is for a warm audience who knows who I am or my building it for, or for a warm audience or for cold. Okay? So that's the first step here, is understanding the traffic temperature. And I would usually break down like hot temperature people who are already your customers or already on your email list. They know who you are. Warm are people who are in your market, but they don't know yet. So again, usually Facebook ads fall in the warm market and then cold is like they have no idea that they even want what you want. For most people I say don't even try to get to cold. It's a lot harder. But we'll talk about that another day. So that's the first phase. Okay? Now after the first phase, then the next thing is what we call our phase number two is our pre-frame bridge. Okay? This is before somebody even gets into our funnel, the frame, what I take them through before they get to my page will change the buying experience. For example, let's say you were on a website and you saw an article that said Russell Brunson is a scam artist. I bought his ClickFunnel thing and I tried to use it and it didn't work. In fact, every time people have money in, it actually took money from me and like I lost all my money and he's horrible, but he's got a new product coming out. So you should click here to check it out, but I wouldn't trust him because I don't. Click here. Okay? That's a pre-frame. That's a frame someone went through before they came to my website. The likelihood that person buying is like zero, right? But if they go to the same page and the page says, hey, I'm a member of Russell Brunson's Inner Circle. I went through all his training. We started with nothing and after that we did this, this and this is our experience of the most amazing thing in the world. He kept his word. He trains good. He's exciting. Like this literally changed my life. His new program came out. You should check it out. And then they go through this new bridge to my page. What's likelihood of them buying now? Way different, right? But it's the exact same funnel. The funnel didn't change over here. Like how come this person is setting up traffic? This person, how come it's working for him and not for them? People don't think about that all the time. They're like, you know, it's not working. I bought ads on Fox News and nobody's buying. It was like, the Fox News are super cold. I have no idea who you are. Okay? But you're warm. Let's buy it like crazy. So you have to understand that. So because of that, these pre-frame bridges here, we need to understand those and understand like where we're sending people through before they come into our funnel. Okay? For example, with warm traffic, it might just be me sending an email. And that email could be the frame that then coming over to my funnel. Okay? If it's warm audience, I might need to get somebody to endorse me. Maybe like, for example, when we launched the, the expert secrets book, I did a Facebook live with Tony Robbins. Tony Robbins introduced me as audience, told me, Russell's awesome. You guys should buy all his, you should buy his book. And then that went out to his audience and that bridge then went, we had like, I don't know, one and a half, two million people have seen that video, then come and bought the books. Like tons of books we sold because he created that connection with his audience and with me, right? And cold is the next phase, but, but it's all about like, what's the, what's the bridge you're going to go through? And as we go through this, these are some of my favorite types of bridges. So we'll get in this in a minute, but these are different types of pre-frame bridges that I would, I would take somebody through. Okay? All right. Now after that, so we take something through this pre-frame bridge. Now the next phase is all this traffic is coming to me. They're hitting a page. And the first thing that I like to do, I want to figure out of all these people who are coming here, who is willing to trade their email address in exchange for information. Okay? I want to qualify my subscribers. Some people are never going to give you the email address. Like, no, I'm not going to do that. And the interesting thing is most of those people who won't give you the email probably won't buy from you either. Okay? So it's important to understand. So the first phase here is like, if all these people come here, let me find out who's willing to trade their email address in exchange for something. So I'm going to have some kind of page there to get their email address. Okay? These are these pages right here. And we're going to talk about these and the strategy behind them. That's the next step is qualifying our subscribers. Okay? After I've qualified my subscribers, my next step is I want to find out of all the people that are coming here, and get this kind of funnel because we have a bunch of people that click, smaller people hit here, like this thing is smaller, like a funnel as it goes through, right? My next thing I want to figure out, like, of all the people who are coming to my page now, who is actually willing to buy something? I want to find out who my buyers are. Dan Kennedy once told me, I can't remember if I read it or if he told me, but he said, you have to understand that someone who buys things continues to buy things. A buyer is a buyer is a buyer. And he started asking me, he's like, what are things that you buy? And I was like, I buy a lot of stuff. Like, I'm passionate about wrestling. I bought a lot of wrestling stuff. I'm passionate about marketing. I buy a lot of marketing stuff. You find out, like, people who are buyers will continue to buy stuff. And I found in my business that people will continue to buy from you until one of two things happen. Number one, you stop selling them stuff, or number two, you offend them. So long as you treat people really, really good, they will continue to keep buying from you for years. If you treat them bad, it's going to be a very short, kind of transactional thing, and it'll be done. But if you treat them, they keep coming back over and over and over and over again, okay? We have a $25,000 coaching program that's a 95% renewal rate. 95% of people pay every single month, every single year, because, boom, because we treat them good, right? And so it's all about that. So I'm trying to qualify my buyers. So I like to sell them something usually on the lower end. That comes right here. These are some of the pages where we sell a product or service on the lower side. So I might sell a free book. I might get them on a webinar where I sell them something. There's different ways to do it, but I'm going to do this step to try to qualify my buyers. Then after I qualify my buyers, they bought something. The next phase, and this kind of goes into the upsells, is like, well, who's really excited and really likes to buy stuff? How many guys are like hyperactive buyers in here? Okay, you're hearing boys here right now with us, so you probably are. So I definitely am. When I get excited about something, I buy a lot of it. Kind of a funny story. I remember, this is probably five or six years ago, we took our entire team to go bowling. I love bowling. It's my third favorite sport. And we're bowling, and I'm doing pretty good. I have my own ball, my own everything. I have fun with it. And then one of the guys that works for me, he beat me at the end. And he was relentless because he knew how much I love bowling. So he made fun of me for a long time. And I was just like, ugh, but I'm trying to be all cool about it. But I was mad. I was in pain. So that night I went home and I started Googling stuff. And I was like, I need to get out of this pain. And I became a hyperactive buyer. So when the first person saw it, I was trying to buy something, and they didn't have anything to buy. I'm like, ugh, sell me something. I want to do something else. And finally I found this place. And I ended up buying a bowling book. I ended up buying a bowling, like a new wrist guard. I bought a new ball. I bought new shoes. I bought all sorts of bowling stuff because I was a hyperactive buyer. I was a hyperactive buyer. I was a buyer in heat. I wanted to solve this problem. And I was buying everything I could. And for a little while there, when people get excited about a topic, they're in heat. They're excited at that time. And they may cool off later. For example, by the time all the bowling stuff showed up, I'd never actually went bowling again with that guy. Even though I wanted to beat him so bad. In fact, I don't think I've ever bowled with most of that stuff. So I forgot about it. But during that period of time, I was excited. And so for me, when someone's buying something, that's what we have upsells and downsells right there. And I had to find people who want to buy more. The first bowling guy sold me a book and that was it. And I had to go find somebody else to buy the ball and somebody else to buy the shoes. So I was giving my money to somebody. And he was doing me a huge disservice. It was a huge pain for me to go find things from all these other people where he could have just had it all for me. If you had made the experience awesome, I bought the book and he's like, hey man, you got this book. This is my favorite ball. Do you want it? I'm like, yeah. It saves me so much time from research and all that kind of stuff. I could have bought the ball. I could have had the shoes. I would have made him a whole bunch more money and it would have served me better as a customer. So I'm finding who my hyperactive buyers are. I serve them at the highest level. They really get exactly what they want. And then from there, this is kind of where the first funnel ends. So the funnel ends. And then as we'll talk about here in a couple of hours, we're going to get into funnel stacking. In the next phase, the funnel is like, okay, what are the next funnels we take them through so we can age and send the relationship? People that join my $25,000 program typically don't just sign up day one and they get into it. Usually they buy a book for me and if they get good value, they're like, what's the next thing he has? Oh, he's got an event over here. And they will see if they receive value every single interaction with me. If they do, they'll keep us sending up. So I have different funnels in place, different things we'll talk about. They help take that experience and keep us sending it so I can serve them at a higher level. I can make more money from them and it makes us a better experience for everybody. And then the last phase here is like taking people offline, like changing the selling environment. And that's kind of, we won't spend too much time in there, but there's other funnels you can take people offline. And that's kind of the last phase here. So those are the seven phases of the funnel that I want you guys to understand because all these things fit somewhere in that process, right? If you got the traffic, if you got the pre-frame bridges, the opt-in pages, the sales pages, the upsells you're going to be doing, the other funnels and things like that as you're kind of going through this process. So this right here is the Funnel Hacker Cookbook. I said, I'm really excited about this. This is actually the first copy of it and it's pretty cool. So as we created this, it wasn't just like we want to make a cookbook to be a cookbook, but I honestly look at what we do with funnel building kind of like a cookbook. That's why I have all these props here. If I was going to go and if I was going to make a cake, I got to get a bowl, but inside of this bowl, there's a whole bunch of ingredients that go into it, right? Now I might get a cake that's like this amazing cake that some baker makes and I eat it. I'm like, oh my gosh, that is amazing. How in the world did he do that? But for the baker, he's just like, look, it's some sugar, some flour, a little bit of this, a little bit of that, dump some milk in, and some vanilla, a shirt like this, a cook of this long, take it out and do this, and then I do this. It's really easy. Second nature for him, right? It's super easy. All he's doing is he's taking all these ingredients that are here in front of him, mixing them together in certain order, certain sequence, and out comes the most amazing cake in the world, right? But as soon as you get a recipe book, this is like, okay, step one, put one fourth cup of this in, you're like, okay, put it in, like cool, you got that done. Step number two now, you need two and a half cups of flour, you dump the flour in, you got that done now. The next one, you're like, all right, you need a whole bunch of butter, you dump the butter in, you dump the chocolate, this probably goes to the end, you put the frosting in, but you go through the whole process, right, and you dump it. Whoa, that was close. The milk and the glass cane that I almost just broke. You dump the milk in, this is like a real cooking show now. You guys loving this? All right. You got the milk, you got the oil, you got the vanilla, boom, and at the end of it, you got this cake, right? Now most of us who aren't, you don't watch the cooking show or stuff like that, who don't understand, like it's magic to us. I ate a cake and I'm like, I don't know how they did that, but wow, that was amazing, right? How do you guys, you look at funnels, right? You look at some of my funnels and you're like, man, Russell's got, I don't even know how much, 15, 18, 20 funnels that we've personally made that made over a million dollars. So he was looking at like, what in the world, how did he do that? But if you understand, it's just ingredients. Like, okay, these are the ingredients that go on this page, these are the pages that go on this funnel, and that's it. And that's what this whole cookbook, I want you guys to understand is that's what this is, like everything's broken down, so you understand what are the ingredients that go into these pages and then what are the pages that go into the funnels. And that's kind of the process we're going to be going through, okay? So the first part of the cookbook as you get into it is what we call the elements. So the elements are literally like the ingredients. This is the flour, this is the milk, this is the butter, this is all the things, okay? Inside of ClickFunnels right now, there's about 50 or so ingredients, and these, we're always adding different ingredients. Like this number will go, get bigger and get smaller, but as of today of us printing this book and making these videos, there's about 50 or so ingredients that are in there. So I'm going to kind of walk through what those ingredients are because I want you to understand that you don't have to like be amazing at building a funnel or building a cake. You say, no, I need some flour, I need some vanilla. Like as long as you know those things are, it's really easy to actually build a cake, right? Same thing here inside the cookbook, okay? So the elements, the first thing, the elements are just like ingredients you would put inside of a recipe. Inside of ClickFunnels, here's your page, this is what you're cooking, right? And building a cake. Over here on the right hand side, these are all of the elements. These are all the ingredients that I put into a page, okay? It may seem mystic, but it's really easy. You're like, this is the headline, so I just grab the headline, boom, and now it's in the page. I'm going to put an image there, so I just grab the image, and that's literally how it works. So these are all of the ingredients that go inside of your cake, okay? And I'm going to kind of walk through these. So there's different types. So the first type of ingredient is called a text element. There's a bunch of different types. So there's a text element that's a headline. So if you need a headline, there's a little element that looks just like this little H, and that sidebar here, right here, you grab it and you drag it, and boom, and the headline pops right there, okay? So there's your headline. Then there's a sub-headline, which is just a smaller headline. You grab that little A, you drag it, boom, now you've got a sub-headline on the page. Okay, you've got a paragraph. This is what the paragraph looks like. It throws a paragraph text in there, and then there's a bullet list, okay? And those are the different, those are the, there's only four, four types of text elements. So if you need text on your page, it's one of those four things, you grab it, you drop it in, okay? Now as we get into these pages, you'll see there's a recipe. So for like the presale page, there, you'll know that every presale page has this, this, and this. Like these are the elements it has to have. And so it's just like a cake you'll see here in a minute. It's just like, okay, for this type of cake, these are the elements I need, okay? So there's all the text elements. The next type of elements are media elements, okay? You can grab these. So the first one here is an image. If you want an image on your page, you just grab the image thing, drop on your page, boom, there's an image there. Next one here is an image pop-up, which means basically, if someone clicks on it, it pops up the image bigger. So if you're like a real estate agent, you got a little picture of a house, you can click on it and show the whole thing or whatever, right? So there's image, there's image pop-up. There's also video, so if you want a video, there's no type of media one, you can drag that in. The video pop-up is basically an image that they click on and it pops up in a video, works really good. And there's a new one that just came out this weekend actually, which is an audio player. So you can grab a little button, boom, there you got an audio player right there on any of your pages, okay? Next after, so we got text, we got media. Next one are form elements. Form elements, there are buttons. So if you want a button like click here, add to car, whatever, there's a button, you grab it, drag it in. Next are input forms. Those are the little fields that people type in email or whatever. So if you want one of those or you want name, email, phone, you just grab three of those things and boom, you got it right there. So you got the inputs. There's select box, which lets you do like selectors. There's text area boxes, so there's different types of form elements, okay? As you play with click phones, you're like, I don't know what this is, you just grab it, drag it on the page and it'll show you and if you don't want that, you can just delete it in like two seconds. So it's really, really easy. It's not like a cake where you dump in the bacon and you just stood up and you're like, girl, bacon cake? Whose idea? Whose amazing idea was that, right? You have no idea. With this, you just drag it in, it's not good, you delete it and you just pull the next one over. So it's really, really forgiving. Then we've got some advanced form elements. We want to have someone sign up with their cell phone to text message. You can throw that on there. Or if you want to capture their cell phone so you can send out text messages, there's a survey element. I'm going to show you guys here in a minute that you can do surveys. You just grab that, boom, and you can do surveys on a page. There's more advanced, more advanced for the shipping address, there's billing address. Now the next one are countdown elements. You want countdown clocks. There's a countdown timer, there's a minute timer, there's a day timer. Okay? And in the book, you can read to find out the difference between the three, but you just grab them, drag them, drop them if you want countdown clocks. Super easy. Next one are content blocks. So if you want icons, you want dividers, you want progress bars, same thing. You grab them, throw them in. There's image lists, there's pricing tables, you grab them, throw them in. Content blocks, again, we've got navigation. So if you want a navigation menu, boom, if you want a frequent last question block, if you want an image feature, throw that in there. Some miscellaneous elements, there's social sharing, so you want to have people share on Facebook, things like that, boom. Facebook comments, again, you can drag and drop it in. Text block, custom code, for those who are more technical and you want custom code, you can throw that in there and throw in JavaScript, HTML, things like that as well. If you're in order form, you can throw in product selectors, you can do order summaries, you can do credit cards, you can do order form bumps. A couple of things, this is the first one we've come upon. Some of these elements are available to all types of pages. And some of these elements only show up in certain things. So if you're a chef and you're like, I know this person's going to try and make a baking cake or a slob's bacon, I'm not even going to give them the option. We're not going to have bacon as an ingredient in his house. I'm like, I can't put bacon in this cake, right? Same thing happens, for example, on an order form over here. When you're editing an order form page, you have to have these things here. So these elements will appear on the page when you're doing an order form page, but they won't show up on any other pages. The same thing if you're doing a webinar registration or you're doing a membership site or different types of pages. There are some of these elements that only appear if that page type allows it. So just know if you're like, I can't find the order form once because you're not on an order form page. So there's order select, order summary, credit card, order form bump. Then you've got the two step order form, which is the element. You've got the order confirmation. For the affiliate centers, you've got user login, affiliate login, affiliate stats, visual funnel. You'll see a bunch of these as we kind of go through the actual pages. You can do images for affiliates, text assets, so solo add things that have affiliates. You can do link builders. Membership sites, you can create login boxes, search boxes that can search in your membership site. You can remember your navigation, the actual membership content. All the members area seems like it's a big fancy thing. It's just basically, okay, we need a user login thing. We need a search bar. We need a navigation and the content. It takes you literally 15 seconds. Your membership site's done. They just got to dump all the content in. This structures how it's going to look. Same with the webinar. You've got an auto webinar registration element. You've got your webinar dates, webinar times, adding the events. I went faster than the elements because you don't need a lot of detail on that. It's just these are the ingredients. This is all the stuff. You need cake, flour, or not cake. You need flour, vanilla, butter, all that stuff. These are the ingredients. As you're going through the cookbook, you can study them. You can learn them. You can log in, click phones, just move them, drag them, and just get comfortable with them. Because in the future, you'll be working on a page like, oh, I wish there was something that did this thing. If you know what those are, you'll know exactly what it is, how to find it, and it'll be really, really easy. That's step number one, are the ingredients. The elements. We call them elements that click funnels. No matter where you are today in your business and in your life, you're just one funnel away.
